preFix.title: Fitting fondant for the winter cluster
preFix.subtitle: A supplement to check stores before the bees are sealed in for winter.
preFix.excerpt: Sugar syrup cannot be cured once temperatures drop below 14°C, and bees will not leave a tight winter cluster to reach stores placed away from them. Fondant goes on top of the cluster and stays accessible all winter. How to choose the right product, how to fit it, and when to check again.
preFix.sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), section on winter preparation. British Beekeepers Association (BBKA), winter management technical notes. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive, chapter on winter preparation. Bee Craft archive, autumn feeding guidance.

content.text: A colony that runs out of stores in February will die before the first spring flowers open. The check to prevent that is straightforward:
hefting
the hive in October tells you whether the stores are adequate. If the hive feels light, a block of
fondant
placed directly above the
winter cluster
will carry them through.
When to check and fit fondant

Heft the hive in late September or October, before the weather turns cold enough to seal the colony in for winter. Lift one side of the hive floor a few centimetres and hold the weight for a moment. A full colony with adequate winter stores is noticeably heavy; a light colony that rises too easily needs supplementary feeding.

Fondant rather than syrup is the right choice from October onward. Below 14°C bees cannot reduce syrup to a curable consistency, and wet uncured syrup ferments in the cell. Fondant stays solid, sits directly above the cluster, and is taken down by the bees as they need it.
What you need
Bakers fondant, 2.5 kg block, or a tray of ready-prepared bakers fondant (plain white sugar paste, not flavoured confectionery fondant)
Crown board to set the fondant on
Bee suit, gloves, and a lit smoker
Hive tool for the propolis seal on the roof

attrs.body: Bakers fondant (also called bakers sugar paste) is plain sucrose in a semi-solid form, available from baking supply shops in 2.5 kg blocks. Confectionery fondant for cake decorating may contain glucose syrup, vanilla, and other additives that are harmless to humans but unsuitable for bees. Check the ingredient list: pure sucrose only.
attrs.title: Use bakers fondant, not confectionery fondant

content.text: Opening the hive

Suit up and light the smoker. On a cold autumn day the colony is less active and a calm approach with one or two puffs of smoke under the roof is all that is needed. Keep the inspection short; the goal is to fit the fondant and close up, not to inspect frames.
Lift the roof and set it aside. Remove the
crown board
and place it on top of the roof, face up. You should be able to see the top of the frames and the beginnings of the cluster position.
Fitting the fondant

Pierce a few small holes in the base of the fondant packaging to allow the bees to access the fondant from below. Place the block directly on top of the frames over the cluster position, packaging side down. The bees will chew through the packaging and into the fondant.

Place the crown board back on top of the fondant block. If the block raises the crown board too high for the roof to fit cleanly, use an eke (an empty super or a purpose-made spacer ring) between the brood box and the crown board to create headroom.
Checking through winter

Check the fondant on a mild day every 4 to 6 weeks through winter. Lift the roof and look; you can usually see how much fondant remains without removing the crown board. A half-eaten block is fine. A block consumed down to the packaging by December means the colony has low stores and a second block should go on immediately.

items.fix: Check the cluster position through the crown board observation window or by brief lift. Reposition the fondant directly over where the bees are clustered.
items.cause: The colony has adequate stores and has not yet needed the supplement, or the fondant was placed away from the cluster

items.fix: Replace with a fresh block immediately. Check again in 4 weeks. If this is a recurring pattern, increase the autumn feeding programme for this hive in future years.
items.cause: Colony went into winter with low stores, or the colony is larger than average and consuming faster

items.fix: Ensure the hive has an upper ventilation gap. A 5 mm prop under the back of the roof is sufficient. A mesh floor also helps; close the varroa insert in the coldest months only.
items.cause: Normal winter cluster respiration; moist warm air rising from the cluster
items.symptom: Condensation pooling on the crown board above the fondant
